participants.name: An (f7ypjp)
participants.professional_persona: An AI research scientist at a local university's AI lab, collaborating with industry partners under the 100E programme to develop custom vision models for manufacturing defect detection. He enjoys bridging the gap between academic research and real-world applications.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Expert in computer vision, generative models, and adversarial robustness; proficient in C++ and Python; published several papers at top conferences (CVPR, NeurIPS).
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Seeks to commercialize his research through a spin-off company and to strengthen Singapore's reputation as a hub for deep tech innovation.

participants.name: Chen (kl1pr7)
participants.professional_persona: Chen Jia Yi is a Research Scientist at A*STAR’s Institute for Infocomm Research, focusing on natural language processing for low-resource languages. She previously worked at a Singaporean fintech startup and brings a pragmatic, product-oriented mindset to her research.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Deep expertise in multilingual NLP, dialogue systems, and transfer learning. Skilled at designing AI prototypes that address specific industry pain points, such as automated customer service for local languages.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Seeks to develop inclusive AI that serves Singapore’s multilingual population. Hopes to commercialize her NLP models through spin-offs or licensing agreements.

participants.name: Jun (t4edo1)
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-t4edo1co
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-t4edo1co
participants.professional_persona: Jun Wei is a young entrepreneur whose startup was selected for the AI Trailblazers programme, receiving free Google Cloud AI tools to build a generative AI platform for personalised education. He is driven by the belief that AI can democratise learning.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Full-stack development, generative AI (LLMs), and product design. Experience with rapid prototyping and cloud deployment.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: He wants to scale his edtech startup internationally, with a mission to provide affordable AI tutoring to students from low-income families in Singapore and beyond.

participants.name: A (60xdmn)
participants.professional_persona: A self-taught AI practitioner who earned his stripes through online courses and hackathons, now working as a data engineer at a fintech startup. He builds scalable data pipelines to support real-time fraud detection models.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Proficient in Apache Spark, Kafka, and cloud-native data platforms (Databricks); hands-on experience with anomaly detection algorithms and feature engineering for tabular data.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Wants to become a lead data engineer and eventually contribute to AI policy discussions, ensuring that smaller companies have access to affordable AI infrastructure.

participants.name: A (76yvyv)
participants.professional_persona: A responsible AI specialist at a global consultancy's Singapore office, ensuring that client AI deployments comply with ethics guidelines and regulatory frameworks. She regularly conducts bias audits and fairness assessments on lending and hiring models.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Deep knowledge of AI ethics frameworks (e.g., Singapore's Model AI Governance Framework), fairness metrics, explainability tools (SHAP, LIME), and risk management practices.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Wants to shape national and international AI governance standards, and to lead a team that advises enterprises on trustworthy AI adoption.

initial_posts.narrative: As an AI governance specialist, I see the government's commitment of over S$500 million for AI R&D as a strong signal to build a robust ecosystem. The co-funding cap of S$330,000 per 100E project is clearly stated in the policy, and it aligns with the need to de-risk enterprise adoption while ensuring meaningful outcomes. For compute infrastructure, I estimate around 500,000 GPU hours based on the scale of the 100E programme and typical allocations for similar national initiatives—this supports about 50-100 projects with moderate compute needs. The trainer count of 50 is plausible given the need for specialized AI experts to guide enterprises and practitioners, as seen in programmes like AIAP and TeSA. These inputs set the stage for generating AI solutions and upskilling the workforce.
evidence: The policy explicitly states 'Over S$500 million committed through AI Singapore for AI R&D', so I take that as the total funding.
The resource logic clearly mentions 'Co-funding up to S$330,000 per project for 100 Experiments', which I use as the max co-funding per project.
Based on typical GPU allocation for national AI programmes (e.g., 10,000 hours per project for 50 projects), 500,000 GPU hours is a reasonable estimate for the initial phase.
The AI Apprenticeship Programme and other training initiatives require dedicated trainers; 50 is a conservative estimate given the need for both technical and governance expertise.

initial_posts.narrative: As a data engineer at a fintech startup, I see the government's S$500 million commitment as a solid foundation for AI R&D, but I'm skeptical about how much of this trickles down to smaller companies like mine. The S$330,000 co-funding cap per 100E project is generous, but I've seen how bureaucratic hurdles can delay access. For compute, 500,000 GPU hours seems reasonable given the 100E programme's scale, but I worry it might be insufficient if demand spikes—my startup struggled to get cloud credits for a fraud detection prototype. The 50 trainers sound adequate for initial upskilling, but I've attended AIAP sessions where trainers were stretched thin. My estimates align with A's post, but I'm less optimistic about execution efficiency.

forward_pass.phase_summary: The group reached strong consensus on all input estimates, with every participant predicting S$500 million total funding, S$330,000 max co-funding per project, 500,000 GPU hours, and 50 trainers, citing explicit policy statements and typical national programme scales. Key themes included the importance of these inputs for de-risking enterprise AI adoption, upskilling the workforce, and supporting 50-100 projects with moderate compute needs. However, several participants expressed skepticism about execution efficiency, noting potential bureaucratic delays, insufficient compute under peak demand, and trainers being stretched thin, which could reduce effective capacity. No numeric disagreements emerged, but the group highlighted that these inputs are only a starting point and that bottlenecks in implementation may limit actual project approvals and deployments.

initial_posts.narrative: Given the S$500 million funding and 50 trainers, I estimate about 30 enterprise outreach sessions to generate problem statements, as each session can engage multiple companies but requires significant preparation. For researcher-enterprise pairings, I expect 15 matchmaking events, roughly one per two outreach sessions, to ensure quality matches. With 50 trainers, I foresee 20 training workshops, each requiring 2-3 trainers, to cover foundational AI skills for enterprises. Platform onboarding assists should be around 100, assuming each of the 50-100 projects needs 1-2 sessions to get started on the AI experimentation platform. These numbers reflect my pragmatic view that execution bottlenecks will limit scale, but the resources are sufficient for a solid start.
evidence: The 50 trainers can support about 20 workshops if each workshop needs 2-3 trainers, consistent with typical AI training programmes I've seen at A*STAR.
Enterprise outreach sessions are time-intensive; 30 sessions is a realistic target for the first year given the need to identify problem statements and build trust.
Matchmaking events require careful pairing of researchers with enterprise challenges, so 15 events aligns with the pace of outreach and researcher availability.
Platform onboarding assists are needed for each project; with 50-100 projects expected, 100 assists covers initial setup and troubleshooting.

initial_posts.narrative: As a young entrepreneur who has gone through the AI Trailblazers programme, I see the government's S$500 million commitment as a strong signal, but I'm acutely aware of execution bottlenecks. With 50 trainers and a focus on quality over quantity, I estimate 25 enterprise outreach sessions to generate problem statements—fewer than Chen's 30 because I think each session requires more preparation and follow-up to yield viable projects. For researcher-enterprise pairings, I expect 12 matchmaking events, roughly one per two outreach sessions, to ensure deep matches rather than superficial ones. Training workshops should be 15, as each workshop needs 2-3 trainers and I've seen trainers stretched thin in practice. Platform onboarding assists should be 80, assuming about 50-100 projects need 1-2 sessions each, but I'm conservative because some enterprises may already have cloud experience from programmes like AI Trailblazers. Chen's estimates are reasonable but slightly optimistic from my perspective—I've seen how bureaucratic delays and trainer fatigue can reduce effective capacity.
